title: CMS NPPES NPI Registry — Provider Enumeration and Status Validation
edition: API v2.1
excerpt: The NPPES NPI Registry is the authoritative source for National Provider Identifier (NPI) validation. A 10-digit NPI is structurally valid if it passes the Luhn check-digit algorithm with the '80840' prefix per CMS NPI enumeration standards. An NPI that is not found in NPPES, or is found with a non-active status, indicates that the provider may not be enrolled with CMS. Billing with a deactivated or invalid NPI results in automatic claim rejection.
notes: Lookup is performed via the NPPES REST API (v2.1) at review time with a 2-second timeout. Network failures do not block claim review. NPI is optional for this MVP — empty NPI fields are silently skipped. Luhn check uses the '80840' prefix per CMS specification.

title: CMS NCCI Medically Unlikely Edits — Practitioner Services (Q3 2026)
excerpt: Medically Unlikely Edits (MUEs) define the maximum units of service a provider would bill for a single beneficiary on a single date of service. MAI 1 = absolute per-line limit (hard denial, cannot be bypassed by modifier or documentation). MAI 2 = date-of-service edit across all claim lines for the same DOS. MAI 3 = date-of-service edit where clinical rationale documentation may allow bypass. Reporting units in excess of the MUE value is a denial trigger for all MAI levels; MAI 1 limits are not bypassable.
notes: Download quarterly from the CMS NCCI MUE page. Place the Practitioner Services MUE xlsx file in data/reference/mue/. Update MUE_VERSION and MUE_EFFECTIVE_DATE in rules/mue_loader.py and restart the application to reload.

title: CMS NCCI Practitioner PTP Edit Files — v322r0 (Effective July 1, 2026)
excerpt: The CMS National Correct Coding Initiative (NCCI) Practitioner PTP edit files define code pairs where one code (Column 2, component) is bundled with another (Column 1, comprehensive). Billing both codes on the same claim constitutes unbundling and will result in denial of the Column 2 code. Modifier indicator 0 = no bypass allowed; indicator 1 = a modifier may allow separate billing if clinically appropriate and documented; indicator 9 = edit not applicable for this pair.
notes: Version v322r0, effective July 1, 2026. Source files: ccipra-v322r0-f1.xlsx through ccipra-v322r0-f4.xlsx (~1.73 million active edit pairs). Update quarterly: download new Practitioner PTP xlsx files from the CMS NCCI page, place in data/reference/ncci/, update NCCI_VERSION and NCCI_EFFECTIVE_DATE in rules/ncci_loader.py, and restart the application.

title: NCCI PTP Edit — Comprehensive Metabolic Panel and Basic Metabolic Panel
section: Physician/Practitioner PTP Edit Table, Column 1 / Column 2
edition: NCCI Policy Manual for Medicare Services (sample reference)
excerpt: CPT code 80048 (Basic Metabolic Panel) is a component of CPT code 80053 (Comprehensive Metabolic Panel). When 80053 is billed, it includes all tests bundled within 80048. Separate billing of 80048 alongside 80053 on the same date of service constitutes unbundling. Modifier indicator 0: no modifier bypass is permitted for this pair.
notes: Modifier indicator 0 — no bypass allowed. Remove 80048 when billing 80053. This PTP edit pair is illustrative of NCCI bundling logic; actual quarterly edit tables are published by CMS.

title: ICD-10-CM Coding Guideline — Z00.00 Encounter for General Adult Medical Examination
section: ICD-10-CM Official Guidelines for Coding and Reporting, Section I.C.21.c — Factors Influencing Health Status (Z Codes)
edition: ICD-10-CM FY2026 (sample reference)
excerpt: Z00.00: Encounter for general adult medical examination without abnormal findings. This code is assigned for a general medical examination that does not reveal any abnormal findings. When a separate problem is encountered and evaluated during the same visit, report an additional code for the condition along with modifier 25 on the associated E/M service. Problem-oriented E/M codes (99202–99215) are not appropriate as the primary code when the encounter is a routine preventive examination unless a separately identifiable service is documented.
notes: Z00.00 signals a well/preventive visit. Pairing it with a problem-oriented E/M without modifier 25 and without a distinct problem diagnosis is a common denial trigger.

title: NCCI Policy Manual — Modifier 25: Significant, Separately Identifiable E/M Service
edition: NCCI Policy Manual for Medicare Services, effective January 2024 (sample reference)
excerpt: Modifier 25 should be appended to an Evaluation and Management (E/M) service code to indicate that on the day of a procedure or service, the patient's condition required a significant, separately identifiable E/M service above and beyond the usual pre- and post-operative care associated with the procedure. The E/M service must be documented separately and must meet the criteria for the level of service billed. Modifier 25 is not appropriate when the E/M service is the only service performed, or when it is provided in the context of a routine preventive examination unless a distinctly different problem is identified and separately addressed.
notes: Modifier 25 is required when a problem-oriented E/M (e.g., 99214) is billed alongside a preventive visit diagnosis (e.g., Z00.00) to signal a separately identifiable service. Without it, the E/M is at risk of denial as duplicative of the preventive visit.

title: CMS Local Coverage Determination — Routine Venipuncture for Collection of Specimens (Illustrative)
edition: LCD Sample Reference (illustrative; not a real LCD number)
excerpt: CPT code 36415 (Collection of venous blood by venipuncture) is separately reimbursable under Medicare when performed in a physician office setting (Place of Service 11) provided the service is medically necessary and documented. When laboratory panel services (e.g., 80048, 80053) are billed on the same date, 36415 is typically payable as the specimen collection mechanism, provided the setting and payer rules permit separate billing. Some payers bundle 36415 into the office visit or panel fee; verify payer-specific policy before billing.
notes: 36415 (venipuncture) is separately billable in POS 11 for Medicare in most cases. Included here as an illustrative LCD-style reference for CLM-001 which bills all four codes together.

title: Medically Unlikely Edits (MUE) — Unit Limits for Laboratory Panel Codes (Illustrative)
section: MUE Table — CPT Codes 80000–89999 (Pathology and Laboratory)
edition: CMS MUE Table Q1 2024 (sample reference)
excerpt: Medically Unlikely Edits (MUEs) define the maximum number of units of service a provider would bill under most circumstances for a single beneficiary on a single date of service. For most laboratory panel codes (80048–80076), the MUE value is 1 unit per date of service. Billing units greater than the MUE value results in an automatic claim line denial. Modifier Adjudication Indicator (MAI) 3 means the MUE is a hard limit based on clinical criteria and cannot be overridden by clinical documentation alone.
notes: MUE limits are published quarterly by CMS. For panel codes, 1 unit per date is the standard limit. This entry is illustrative of MUE logic for the synthetic claim set.

title: Local Coverage Determination — Medical Necessity for Problem-Oriented E/M Services Billed with Preventive Diagnosis (Illustrative)
section: Indications and Limitations of Coverage and/or Medical Necessity
edition: LCD Sample Reference (illustrative; based on CMS LCD policy principles)
excerpt: Problem-oriented Evaluation and Management (E/M) services (CPT 99202–99215) billed with a primary diagnosis of Z00.00 (Encounter for general adult medical examination without abnormal findings) are subject to medical necessity review. When the primary reason for the encounter is a routine preventive examination (Z00.xx), a separately identifiable problem-oriented E/M service requires: (1) documentation of a distinct medical condition addressed during the visit, (2) a separate problem diagnosis code beyond the preventive diagnosis, and (3) modifier 25 appended to the E/M code. Failure to meet these requirements may result in denial of the problem-oriented E/M as not medically necessary or as a duplicate of the preventive service. Payers may require that the separate condition be a new or worsening problem, not a chronic stable condition routinely monitored at preventive visits.
notes: This LCD-style reference applies when Z00.00 is the primary or only diagnosis on a claim containing problem-oriented E/M codes. Coverage concern is triggered when no separate problem diagnosis is present alongside the preventive diagnosis, or when modifier 25 is absent. Designed for use with coverage validation agent v1.

title: Local Coverage Determination — Medical Necessity for E/M Services: Hypertension Management (Illustrative)
edition: LCD Sample Reference (illustrative; based on CMS LCD policy principles)
excerpt: Evaluation and Management (E/M) services (CPT 99202–99215) are considered medically necessary when performed for the evaluation, management, or monitoring of Essential Hypertension (ICD-10-CM I10). Hypertension is a chronic condition that frequently requires ongoing clinical assessment, medication management, and complication screening. Office or outpatient E/M services billed with a primary diagnosis of I10 are generally supported as medically necessary when the visit documentation reflects blood pressure assessment, medication review or adjustment, and/or evaluation for target organ damage. No additional LCD restrictions apply to routine hypertension management E/M visits when adequately documented.
notes: I10 is a well-supported indication for E/M services. Coverage validation agent should return no concern for I10-only claims with appropriate E/M codes. Designed as the 'no concern' test case for coverage validation agent v1.

title: Local Coverage Determination — Preventive Medicine Services: Periodic Comprehensive Preventive Visit, Ages 18–39 (Illustrative)
edition: LCD Sample Reference (illustrative; based on CMS LCD policy principles)
excerpt: CPT code 99395 (Periodic comprehensive preventive medicine reevaluation and management of an individual, 18–39 years) is covered for established patients presenting for a routine annual preventive examination. Appropriate diagnosis codes include Z00.00 (Encounter for general adult medical examination without abnormal findings) and Z00.01 (Encounter for general adult medical examination with abnormal findings). Coverage is limited to once per calendar year per beneficiary. Services must include a comprehensive history, physical examination, and anticipatory guidance appropriate for the patient's age and risk factors. When Z00.01 is used, documentation must reflect the abnormal finding(s) identified. Additional problem-oriented E/M services on the same date require modifier 25 and a separate problem diagnosis.
notes: 99395 is the preventive visit code for patients aged 18–39. Coverage is conditional on annual frequency and appropriate diagnosis. Modifier 25 rules apply if a problem E/M is also billed. Designed for use with coverage validation agent v1.

title: Local Coverage Determination — Medical Necessity for E/M Services: Diabetes Mellitus Management (Illustrative)
edition: LCD Sample Reference (illustrative; based on CMS LCD policy principles)
excerpt: Evaluation and Management (E/M) services (CPT 99202–99215) are considered medically necessary when performed for the management of Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (ICD-10-CM E11.xx) and related complications. Covered indications include glycemic management (E11.9), diabetic peripheral neuropathy (E11.40), hypoglycemia (E11.65), and other diabetic complications (E13.9). Visit documentation must reflect glucose monitoring results, medication review or adjustment, dietary and lifestyle counseling, and assessment of complications or comorbidities. Frequency of visits is expected to align with clinical complexity: patients with uncontrolled diabetes may require quarterly visits; stable, well-controlled patients are generally seen one to two times per year. Ordering of HbA1c (CPT 83036) up to four times per year is supported when diabetes management is actively documented.
notes: E11.x codes are well-supported indications for E/M services. Coverage validation agent should return no concern for standard diabetes management claims unless complication documentation is absent. Supports HbA1c frequency validation (see LCD_HEMOGLOBIN_A1C_FREQUENCY).

title: Local Coverage Determination — Medical Necessity for E/M Services: Dyslipidemia Management (Illustrative)
edition: LCD Sample Reference (illustrative; based on CMS LCD policy principles)
excerpt: E/M services for management of dyslipidemia (ICD-10-CM E78.5 Hyperlipidemia, unspecified; E78.00 Pure hypercholesterolemia, unspecified; E78.01 Familial hypercholesterolemia; E78.2 Mixed hyperlipidemia; E78.4 Other hyperlipidemia) are covered under Medicare Part B when documented clinical management is provided. Documentation must reflect current lipid values, medication regimen review, cardiovascular risk assessment, and patient counseling regarding dietary and lifestyle modifications. Lipid panel monitoring (CPT 80061) is expected annually for patients on stable statin therapy; more frequent monitoring is supported when initiating or changing lipid-lowering therapy or when clinical instability is documented. Claims for dyslipidemia management E/M without current lipid values or medication documentation may be subject to medical necessity review.
notes: Dyslipidemia is a covered indication for ongoing E/M services. Pair with LCD_LIPID_PANEL_FREQUENCY for complete lipid management policy coverage. Designed for demo scenario: lipid management claim with E78.5 + 99214.

title: Local Coverage Determination — Medical Necessity for Metabolic Panel Laboratory Tests (Illustrative)
section: Indications and Limitations of Coverage and/or Medical Necessity
edition: LCD Sample Reference (illustrative; based on CMS LCD policy principles)
excerpt: Metabolic panel laboratory tests — including the Comprehensive Metabolic Panel (CPT 80053), Basic Metabolic Panel (CPT 80048), and Hepatic Function Panel (CPT 80076) — are covered under Medicare Part B when ordered for the evaluation, monitoring, or management of a documented clinical condition. Supported indications include chronic disease monitoring (diabetes mellitus, hypertension, chronic kidney disease, heart failure), medication management requiring electrolyte or renal function monitoring, and evaluation of new or worsening symptoms with suspected metabolic etiology. Laboratory tests ordered solely in connection with a routine wellness or preventive examination, in the absence of a documented clinical condition requiring monitoring, are generally not covered under Medicare Part B and are the patient's financial responsibility. When ordering metabolic panels during a preventive visit, providers must document a distinct clinical indication beyond the preventive encounter diagnosis (Z00.xx) to support medical necessity.
notes: This is the primary trigger policy for the 'labs with well-visit diagnosis only' denial scenario. When 80053 or 80048 are billed with only Z00.xx diagnoses and no chronic condition codes, this policy supports a MEDIUM-severity coverage concern. Pairs with LCD_VENIPUNCTURE_36415_SAMPLE.

title: Local Coverage Determination — Lipid Panel Frequency Limitations for Medicare Beneficiaries (Illustrative)
edition: LCD Sample Reference (illustrative; based on CMS LCD policy principles)
excerpt: Medicare Part B covers lipid panel testing (CPT 80061 Lipid panel; CPT 82465 Cholesterol, serum or whole blood, total) when ordered for diagnosis or monitoring of dyslipidemia, cardiovascular disease risk, or other covered indications. For beneficiaries on stable lipid-lowering therapy with documented therapeutic response, coverage is supported for lipid panel testing once per calendar year. More frequent testing — up to every three to six months — is supported when: (1) lipid-lowering therapy is being initiated or significantly modified, (2) lipid values are outside therapeutic targets, (3) adherence or adverse effects require active monitoring, or (4) a new cardiovascular event or diagnosis is documented. Routine annual testing performed solely as part of a general wellness examination, without a documented dyslipidemia or cardiovascular risk diagnosis, is not covered under Medicare Part B and requires an Advance Beneficiary Notice (ABN) if the patient requests the service.
notes: Lipid panels are subject to frequency restrictions under Medicare. Claims with E78.xx diagnoses and 80061 generally pass; claims with only Z00.xx require ABN and may be denied. Designed for demo: 80061 ordered for a patient on stable statin therapy — once/year is fine, twice in six months requires documentation.

title: Local Coverage Determination — Glycosylated Hemoglobin (HbA1c) Testing Frequency for Diabetes Management (Illustrative)
section: Indications, Frequency Limitations, and Coverage Criteria
edition: LCD Sample Reference (illustrative; based on CMS LCD policy principles)
excerpt: Glycosylated Hemoglobin (HbA1c) testing (CPT 83036; CPT 83037) is covered under Medicare Part B for beneficiaries with a confirmed diagnosis of diabetes mellitus (ICD-10-CM E10.xx, E11.xx, E13.xx) or pre-diabetes (R73.03) when ordered for the purpose of glycemic monitoring and diabetes management. Standard coverage supports up to four HbA1c tests per calendar year for patients with established diabetes. More than four tests per year requires documentation of clinical instability, frequent medication adjustments, or a specific clinical rationale for increased monitoring frequency. HbA1c testing in beneficiaries without a documented diabetes or pre-diabetes diagnosis is not covered as a screening test under Part B; such screening, if appropriate, is covered under the Medicare Diabetes Prevention Program (MDPP) or specific preventive benefit provisions. A diagnosis of Z00.00 or Z00.01 alone does not support coverage for HbA1c testing.
notes: HbA1c is CPT-indexed here (not diagnosis-indexed) so it is retrieved when 83036 is on the claim. Coverage concern triggers when no E11.xx or E13.xx diagnosis is present, or when frequency exceeds 4x/year without documentation. Demo scenario: 83036 + E11.9 = no concern; 83036 + Z00.00 alone = concern.

title: Local Coverage Determination — Preventive Medicine Services: Periodic Comprehensive Preventive Visit, Ages 40–64 (Illustrative)
edition: LCD Sample Reference (illustrative; based on CMS LCD policy principles)
excerpt: CPT code 99396 (Periodic comprehensive preventive medicine reevaluation and management of an individual, 40–64 years) is covered for established patients presenting for a routine annual preventive examination. Appropriate primary diagnosis codes include Z00.00 (Encounter for general adult medical examination without abnormal findings) and Z00.01 (Encounter for general adult medical examination with abnormal findings). Coverage is limited to once per calendar year per beneficiary. For Medicare beneficiaries enrolled in traditional Medicare (Parts A and B), the Annual Wellness Visit (AWV) benefit — billed as G0438 (initial AWV) or G0439 (subsequent AWV) — is the preferred preventive benefit and is a covered Part B benefit without cost-sharing. Commercial preventive visit codes 99395–99397 are not a covered Medicare Part B benefit in most cases; beneficiaries should be informed of potential out-of-pocket liability. When a problem-oriented E/M is also provided on the same date as a preventive visit, modifier 25 and a separate problem diagnosis are required for the E/M to be separately payable.
notes: 99396 is the preventive visit code for patients aged 40–64. Note that traditional Medicare generally does not cover 99395–99397 as a benefit — the AWV (G0438/G0439) is the Medicare preventive visit equivalent. This policy is relevant for Medicare Advantage plans that may cover 99396. Pairs with NCD_AWV_G0438_G0439.

title: National Coverage Determination — Medicare Annual Wellness Visit (AWV): G0438 and G0439 (Illustrative)
edition: NCD Sample Reference (illustrative; based on Medicare Benefit Policy Manual, Chapter 15, Section 280.5)
excerpt: The Medicare Annual Wellness Visit (AWV) is a covered Part B preventive benefit. G0438 (Initial Preventive Physical Examination / Welcome to Medicare Visit, when not already performed, or Initial AWV) and G0439 (Subsequent AWV) are covered once per calendar year (12 full months must have elapsed between AWV services). The AWV includes a Health Risk Assessment (HRA), review of medical and family history, establishment of a Prevention Plan, and cognitive screening. The AWV does not include a physical examination in the traditional sense and is not equivalent to a problem-oriented visit. When a problem-oriented E/M service (CPT 99202–99215) is provided on the same date as an AWV for a separately identifiable medical concern, the E/M must be billed with modifier 25 and a distinct problem diagnosis to be separately payable. Without modifier 25, the E/M will be denied as bundled with the AWV. AWV and routine preventive visit codes (99395–99397) may not be billed on the same date of service.
notes: Primary trigger for the 'AWV + same-day E/M without modifier 25' denial scenario. Modifier 25 concern is HIGH severity when G0438 or G0439 is present with an E/M on the same date without the modifier. Demo scenario: G0439 + 99213 on same date, no modifier 25 documented.

title: National Coverage Determination — Colorectal Cancer Screening: Colonoscopy (Illustrative; based on NCD 210.3)
section: Coverage Criteria, Frequency Limitations, and Screening-to-Diagnostic Conversion
edition: NCD Sample Reference (illustrative; based on CMS NCD 210.3 — Colorectal Cancer Screening)
excerpt: Medicare covers screening colonoscopy (CPT 45378) for beneficiaries who are: (1) at average risk, once every 10 years (120 months); (2) at high risk (personal or family history of colorectal cancer or adenomatous polyps; inflammatory bowel disease), once every 24 months (2 years). High-risk status must be documented using appropriate diagnosis codes (Z80.0 Family history of malignant neoplasm of digestive organs; Z83.71 Family history of colonic polyps; K51.xx Ulcerative colitis; K50.xx Crohn's disease). When a screening colonoscopy results in the removal of a polyp or lesion — requiring therapeutic procedures such as 45380 (biopsy), 45385 (polypectomy with snare technique), or 45388 (ablation) — the encounter converts from screening to diagnostic. In this case, the primary diagnosis must be updated from Z12.11 (Encounter for screening for malignant neoplasm of colon) to reflect the actual finding (e.g., K63.5 Polyp of colon). Failure to update the diagnosis may result in denial of the therapeutic procedure codes, as therapeutic procedures are not separately reimbursable under the screening benefit.
notes: Primary trigger for the 'screening colonoscopy converts to diagnostic' denial scenario. When 45385 (polypectomy) is billed alongside Z12.11 with no finding diagnosis, a MEDIUM-HIGH coverage concern is appropriate. Screening interval violations (e.g., second colonoscopy within 10 years without high-risk documentation) are also flagged.

title: Local Coverage Determination — Diagnosis Code Requirements for Colonoscopy: Screening vs. Diagnostic Distinction (Illustrative)
section: ICD-10-CM Diagnosis Code Guidance and Coverage Limitations
edition: LCD Sample Reference (illustrative; based on CMS LCD policy principles)
excerpt: Accurate ICD-10-CM diagnosis coding is required to distinguish screening colonoscopy from diagnostic colonoscopy, as each is subject to different coverage rules, patient cost-sharing, and frequency limitations. Z12.11 (Encounter for screening for malignant neoplasm of colon) designates a purely screening encounter and supports coverage under the colorectal cancer screening benefit (CPT 45378 at screening frequency). Z12.12 (Encounter for screening for malignant neoplasm of rectum) applies similarly. When a colonoscopy is performed due to symptoms — rectal bleeding, change in bowel habits, iron deficiency anemia, positive fecal occult blood test — a symptom or condition code must be used as the primary diagnosis (e.g., K92.1 Melena, K57.30 Diverticulosis, R19.5 Other fecal abnormalities), not a screening code. Family history codes (Z80.0 Family history of malignant neoplasm of digestive organs; Z83.71 Family history of colonic polyps) support high-risk screening frequency but do not convert the encounter to diagnostic. Miscoding a diagnostic colonoscopy as screening, or a screening colonoscopy that included polypectomy without diagnosis update, are among the most common audit findings for endoscopy services.
notes: Diagnosis-indexed policy retrieved when Z12.11 or family history codes are on the claim. Pairs with NCD_COLORECTAL_SCREENING_COLONOSCOPY. Supports detection of: (1) screening-to-diagnostic conversion failures when polypectomy is billed; (2) frequency violations without adequate high-risk documentation.

title: National Coverage Determination — Mammography: Screening and Diagnostic (Illustrative; based on NCD 220.4 and 220.5)
edition: NCD Sample Reference (illustrative; based on CMS NCD 220.4 — Screening Mammography and NCD 220.5 — Diagnostic Mammography)
excerpt: Screening mammography (CPT 77067 Digital bilateral screening mammography) is covered annually for female Medicare beneficiaries age 40 and older. Coverage requires assignment of a screening diagnosis: Z12.31 (Encounter for screening mammogram for malignant neoplasm of breast) is the appropriate code. Screening and diagnostic mammography may not be billed on the same date of service for the same patient. Diagnostic mammography (CPT 77065 Diagnostic mammography, including CAD, unilateral; CPT 77066 Diagnostic mammography, including CAD, bilateral) is covered when there is a specific clinical indication: a palpable mass, nipple discharge, skin changes, asymmetric density on prior screening, or an abnormal screening result (e.g., BI-RADS 0, 3, 4, or 5). Diagnostic mammography requires a symptom or finding code as the primary diagnosis (e.g., N63.10 Unspecified lump in unspecified part of right breast; R92.0 Mammographic microcalcification found on diagnostic imaging). Billing 77065 or 77066 with only a screening diagnosis (Z12.31 or Z12.39) without a documented clinical indication constitutes a coding error and will result in claim denial.
notes: Covers both screening (77067) and diagnostic (77065/77066) mammography. Primary concern scenarios: (1) diagnostic mammography billed with only a screening diagnosis; (2) both screening and diagnostic billed same date. Demo scenario: 77066 + Z12.31 without a finding code = MEDIUM coverage concern.

title: Local Coverage Determination — Hospital Observation Services: Medical Necessity and Documentation Requirements (Illustrative)
section: Indications and Limitations of Coverage and/or Medical Necessity
edition: LCD Sample Reference (illustrative; based on CMS LCD policy principles and Medicare Benefit Policy Manual Chapter 6)
excerpt: Hospital observation services (CPT 99234–99236 Observation or inpatient care, same-day admit and discharge; HCPCS G0379 Direct referral for hospital observation) are covered under Medicare Part B when the treating physician has ordered observation status and documented clinical reasons why the patient requires continuous monitoring and assessment but does not meet criteria for inpatient admission. Observation is appropriate when the patient's condition is: (1) uncertain and requiring monitoring before a determination can be made regarding inpatient admission or discharge, (2) responding to treatment in a manner that may permit discharge within a short period, or (3) at risk for complications requiring immediate clinical response. Observation services of less than eight hours are generally not reimbursable unless the patient is discharged home in less than eight hours and documentation supports the clinical necessity of the observation period. Concurrent inpatient admission and observation billing is not permitted. Claims for observation that do not meet the above criteria, or lack a physician order for observation status, are subject to denial and post-payment audit.
notes: Observation billing is a common audit target. Concern is triggered when observation is billed without adequate documentation of monitoring necessity, or when duration is insufficient. Designed for demo scenario: inpatient-level care billed as observation, or observation without physician order documented.

title: Local Coverage Determination — ICD-10-CM Diagnosis Code Specificity Requirements (Illustrative)
edition: LCD Sample Reference (illustrative; based on ICD-10-CM Official Guidelines for Coding and Reporting and CMS LCD policy principles)
excerpt: Per ICD-10-CM Official Guidelines for Coding and Reporting, codes must be used to the highest degree of specificity that is supported by the medical record documentation. Unspecified codes — those with descriptors such as 'unspecified,' 'not otherwise specified (NOS),' or 'other' — are appropriate only when the provider's documentation does not permit selection of a more specific code. Commonly flagged unspecified codes in the context of musculoskeletal and pain management claims include: M54.9 (Dorsalgia, unspecified), M79.3 (Panniculitis, unspecified), and M25.50 (Pain in unspecified joint). When documentation supports anatomical specificity — for example, lumbar region (M54.5 Low back pain), cervical region (M54.2 Cervicalgia), or a specific joint — the unspecified code should not be used. Use of unspecified codes when more specific codes are available constitutes incomplete coding and may: (1) trigger Additional Documentation Requests (ADRs) from payers, (2) result in denial of the claim for insufficient clinical information, and (3) be identified as a compliance concern in RAC or OIG audit programs. Similarly, R51.9 (Headache, unspecified) and R05.9 (Cough, unspecified) have specificity alternatives that should be used when documentation permits.
notes: Diagnosis-indexed policy retrieved when unspecified ICD-10 codes are on the claim. Concern is MEDIUM severity — flagging specificity issues does not constitute a hard denial but signals a documentation improvement opportunity that reduces audit risk. Demo scenario: 99215 + M54.9 without anatomical specification in the note.

title: Local Coverage Determination — Chronic Care Management (CCM) Services: Requirements and Limitations (Illustrative)
edition: LCD Sample Reference (illustrative; based on CMS Chronic Care Management Services MLN Matters SE1516 and CY2024 PFS Final Rule)
excerpt: Chronic Care Management (CCM) services (CPT 99490 CCM services, at least 20 minutes of clinical staff time; CPT 99491 CCM services, at least 30 minutes of physician/QHP time; HCPCS G0511 Rural health clinic or federally qualified health center CCM) are covered under Medicare Part B for beneficiaries with two or more chronic conditions expected to last at least 12 months or until the death of the patient, and that place the patient at significant risk of death, acute exacerbation/decompensation, or functional decline. Required elements for coverage include: (1) an initiating visit or consent obtained and documented, (2) a written individualized care plan developed, implemented, revised, or monitored during the billing period, (3) 24/7 access to the care team for urgent needs, (4) care coordination among treating providers, and (5) at least 20 minutes (99490) or 30 minutes (99491) of non-face-to-face clinical staff or physician time documented with start/stop times or total duration in the calendar month. CCM may not be billed in the same month as: Transitional Care Management (99495, 99496), behavioral health integration services (99484, 99492, 99493, 99494), or Principal Care Management (99424, 99425, 99426, 99427). Only one CCM code may be billed per beneficiary per calendar month.
notes: CCM billing is high-risk for audit due to documentation requirements. Concern triggers when: (1) fewer than 2 chronic conditions documented, (2) care plan not referenced, (3) time documentation missing, (4) conflicting care management code in same month. Demo scenario: 99490 billed for a patient with only one documented chronic condition.

title: Local Coverage Determination — E/M Office Visit Level Documentation: Time-Based vs. Medical Decision Making (Illustrative)
section: Coding Level Support Requirements under 2021 AMA E/M Guidelines
edition: LCD Sample Reference (illustrative; based on AMA CPT E/M Guidelines effective January 2021 and CMS adoption in CY2021 PFS Final Rule)
excerpt: Effective January 1, 2021, office and outpatient E/M visit levels (CPT 99202–99205 new patient; 99211–99215 established patient) are determined by either (1) total time on the date of the encounter, or (2) medical decision making (MDM). Selection of the coding method is made by the reporting provider; however, the method chosen must be consistently documented within the encounter note. Time-based coding: Total time includes face-to-face and non-face-to-face activities on the date of service (e.g., reviewing results, communicating with other providers, documenting). The note must state the total time spent. MDM-based coding: The level is determined by the intersection of Number and Complexity of Problems, Amount and/or Complexity of Data Reviewed, and Risk of Complications and/or Morbidity or Mortality. A high-complexity MDM (99215, 99205) requires at least two of the following: (a) multiple chronic illnesses with severe exacerbation or new problem requiring additional workup, (b) review of extensive data including independent interpretation of imaging/labs, (c) high-risk management decision such as drug therapy requiring intensive monitoring. Mismatch between the billed E/M level and the documented MDM or time is among the most frequently cited findings in payer audit programs. Claims for 99214–99215 with documentation supporting only a lower level are subject to downcoding and overpayment recovery.
notes: E/M level documentation is a universal concern for all office visits. Concern triggers when documentation language suggests lower complexity than billed level (e.g., 99215 with routine follow-up language only). Also retrieved for claims with 99213/99214 to flag potential underdocumentation. Demo scenario: 99215 + M54.9 where note lacks MDM documentation to support high complexity.
